coredata.dc:title: Hollow spherical biomass derived-carbon dotted with SnS2/g-C3N4 Z-scheme heterojunction for efficient CO2 photoreduction into CO
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yang
Yin, Qiao
Zeng, Yingshan
Liu, Zhi
coredata.dc:description: The core of realizing the effective CO2 conversion by solar energy is to develop a stable photocatalyst with high CO2 adsorption capacity and high charge separation efficiency. In this work, a hollow spherical Z-scheme SnS2/g-C3N4/C (SCC) photocatalyst is successfully constructed by a morphology-inherited strategy. Biomass mabospores are utilized as both the precursor for formation of hollow spherical structure and carbon source, and the g-C3N4 and SnS2 nanosheets tightly anchor on the surface of mabospores-derived carbon spheres. The Z-scheme SCC heterostructure exhibits 5.5 times activity enhancement on CO2 photoreduction to CO with a high evolution rate up to 40.86 μmol∙g−1∙h−1. Examined by various photoelectrochemical analysis, the boosted photocatalytic activity originates mainly from the construction of hollow spherical Z-scheme heterojunction, providing the more preferable basic sites for CO2 adsorption, promoting the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs, as well as increasing the reductionability of electrons in conduction band of g-C3N4. The formed internal electric fields between the g-C3N4 and SnS2 can also further reinforce the spatial separation of photogenerated charge carriers. Theoretical calculations further verify that the SCC Z-scheme heterostructure can effectively decrease the energy barriers of formed intermediates, especially the crucial COOH*, and thus leading to the facile conversion of CO2-to-CO. This work induces a new strategy to construct g-C3N4-based Z-scheme heterojunction assisted by versatile biomasses with optimized structure and enhanced photoelectrochemical merits for efficient CO2 conversion into chemical fuels.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4
SnS2
Biomass-derived carbon
CO2 photoreduction
Theoretical calculation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns (a) and FT-IR (b) of the as-prepared samples, XPS spectra of the SCC: C 1 s (c), N 1 s (d), S 2p (e), Sn 3d (f).
SEM images of the SCC with increasing magnification (a-d) and the corresponding EDS line scan (e and f).
TEM images (a-d), HRTEM image (e), SAED mode (f), and HAADF-STEM with elemental mapping images of C, N, Sn, and S on the SCC (g-l).
CO2-TPD (a) and EPR spectra (b) of the as-prepared samples.
TPR (a), EIS (b), PL spectra (c) and fitted TRPL curves (d) of the as-prepared samples.
CO yields of various photocatalysts (a, b), CO-TPD profiles (c), and isotope analysis of 13CO with13CO2 as the raw gas for the SCC (d).
ESR spectra for DMPO-•O2– (a) and DMPO-•OH (b).
UPS spectra of the g-C3N4 (a) and SnS2 (b), sketch of charge transfer (c), and the calculated band structure of g-C3N 4(d) and SnS2 (e).

In situ DRIFTS spectra of photocatalytic CO2 reduction with H2O over the SCC with increasing irradiation time (a), the adsorption energies of CO2, COOH* and CO* adsorbed on g-C3N4 and SCC (b), proposed reaction pathways of CO2 photoreduction on g-C3N4 (c) and SCC (d), and calculated Gibbs free energy diagrams for intermediates on g-C3N4 and SCC (e).
Proposed mechanism on photocatalytic reduction of CO2 into CO on the SCC.
Potential CO2 photoreduction process over the SCC from in situ DRIFTS.

head.ce:title: Hollow spherical biomass derived-carbon dotted with SnS2/g-C3N4 Z-scheme heterojunction for efficient CO2 photoreduction into CO

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shantou University, Shantou 515063, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shantou University, Shantou 515063, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hollow spherical configuration, facilitating internal electric field (IEF), highly-dispersed Z-scheme SnS2/g-C3N4 heterostructure, and increased basic sites for CO2 adsorption, endow the SnS2/g-C3N4/C (SCC) photocatalyst prominent photocatalytic activity of CO2-to-CO.

The core of realizing the effective CO2 conversion by solar energy is to develop a stable photocatalyst with high CO2 adsorption capacity and high charge separation efficiency. In this work, a hollow spherical Z-scheme SnS2/g-C3N4/C (SCC) photocatalyst is successfully constructed by a morphology-inherited strategy. Biomass mabospores are utilized as both the precursor for formation of hollow spherical structure and carbon source, and the g-C3N4 and SnS2 nanosheets tightly anchor on the surface of mabospores-derived carbon spheres. The Z-scheme SCC heterostructure exhibits 5.5 times activity enhancement on CO2 photoreduction to CO with a high evolution rate up to 40.86 μmol∙g−1∙h−1. Examined by various photoelectrochemical analysis, the boosted photocatalytic activity originates mainly from the construction of hollow spherical Z-scheme heterojunction, providing the more preferable basic sites for CO2 adsorption, promoting the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs, as well as increasing the reductionability of electrons in conduction band of g-C3N4. The formed internal electric fields between the g-C3N4 and SnS2 can also further reinforce the spatial separation of photogenerated charge carriers. Theoretical calculations further verify that the SCC Z-scheme heterostructure can effectively decrease the energy barriers of formed intermediates, especially the crucial COOH*, and thus leading to the facile conversion of CO2-to-CO. This work induces a new strategy to construct g-C3N4-based Z-scheme heterojunction assisted by versatile biomasses with optimized structure and enhanced photoelectrochemical merits for efficient CO2 conversion into chemical fuels.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoreduction of CO2 to added-value chemical fuels (CO, CH4, CH3OH, etc) driven by sunlight from the simulation of ‘photosynthesis' has been regarded as a green and sustainable energy conversion strategy to alleviate the pressing crisis between energy and environment [1]. Developing efficient and stable photocatalysts is the kernel of this strategy. Semiconductor materials have shown excellent application prospects in CO2 photoreduction, and the whole reaction process can be accomplished under mild conditions [2,3]. Amongst the developing photocatalysts, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has triggered extensive interests at present because of its unique merits, including reasonable energy band structure, suitable band gap (∼2.7 eV), good stability, non-toxicity, and facile synthesis [4,5]. Actually, the pristine g-C3N4 always suffers from low activity for the CO2 photoreduction, mainly stemming from low specific surface area, limited visible light adsorption, especially high recombination rate of photoinduced electrons and holes [6-8]. Therefore, it has being highly desirable for pristine g-C3N4 to explore effective strategy and modify photoelectrochemical characteristics to obtain better photocatalytic performance. Based on the aim, a variety of regulation methods has been attempted, including the construction of more active sites on the morphology and structure, noble-metal modified, heterojunction formed, co-catalyst introduced, etc [9-12]. Recently, carbonaceous materials are used to integrate with g-C3N4 (g-C3N4/C) to increase the dispersion of active sites and prolong the lifetime of photogenerated electrons. Xu et al. construct an Ag2CrO4/g-C3N4/graphene oxide Z-scheme nanocomposite using graphene oxide as carbon source, which delivers 2.3 times increase of CO2 conversion activity compared with that of the pristine g-C3N4 [13]. Jo et al. choose carbon dots to the fabricate a N-doped C dot/CoAl-layered double hydroxide/g-C3N4 hybrid heterojunction for enhanced selective reduction of CO2 into CH4 under simulated-solar-light illumination in the water [14]. Song et al. prepare carbon and oxygen co-doped g-C3N4 with an intra-molecular donor acceptor system, effectively reducing electron-hole recombination rate [15]. As a developed renewable carbon source, biomass-derived carbon (BC) has been considered to be coupled with various semiconductor(s) in photocatalysis due to its outstanding characteristics such as diversified nanostructure, high conductivity, light corrosion resistance, high specific surface area, etc [16-18]. Li et al. obtain biochar skeleton and “chrysanthemum” g-C3N4 by one-step co-heat treatment using cellulose as template, which increases the specific surface area of carbon nitrous oxide and improves the efficiency of charge separation for photocatalytic degradation of formaldehyde [19]. Xu et al. use sawdust as raw material to get a nitrogen-doped biochar with abundant defects, which can work at a wide pH range and high salinity conditions for the degradation of organic pollutants [20]. In a variety of microscopic morphologies, the hollow spherical structure has been much reported with unique advantages for photocatalysis, including excellent photoelectric properties, multiple scattering of light for light absorption and utilization, high specific surface area with abundant active sites, and large light absorption area [21,22]. Therefore, it is reasonable to conjecture that constructing a g-C3N4/BC photocatalyst with hollow spherical morphology is an effective means to improve the photocatalytic activity.

Tin disulphide (SnS2) is another naturally non-poisonous semiconductor with a narrow band gap (∼2.2 eV), which makes it an excellent candidate to integrate with the host semiconductor to construct a heterostructure for enhanced photocatalytic performance. Chen et al. report that a SnO2@SnS2 heterojunction used for the degradation of methyl orange, which plays important roles in promoting interfacial electron transfer and reducing charge self-radiation recombination for SnO2 [23]. Sun et al. prepare a SnS2/TiO2 nano-heterojunction by means of a two-step hydrothermal route, which shortens the charge transport distance, resulting in a higher photocatalytic activity of H2 evolution for TiO2 [24]. In addition, it has been much reported that SnS2 has good light sensitivity with more harvest of light energy, demonstrating great potentials in dye-sensitized solar cells, gas sensors, photovoltaic elements, as well as energy conversion and storage in the past years [25,26]. Albeit these progress, there are rare reports to date concerning on combining the structural and photoelectrochemical advantages to construct a photocatalyst, which is composed of g-C3N4, SnS2, and BC for enhanced performance of CO2 photoreduction.

The fresh puffball spores are cleaned and sieved to pick up well-proportioned mabospores. Then, 5.0 g of the sieved mabospores are soaked and stirred in 0.7 M KOH solution for 12 h. Afterwards, the mixture was dried at 80 °C, and then dispered in 0.5 M HCl solution for 1 h to neutralize the residual KOH, followed rinsing with deionized water to neutrality. After drying at 60 °C overnight, the pretreated spore precursor (PSP) with hollow spherical structure was obtained, as shown in Fig. S2 .

2.1 g of SnCl4·5H2O and 3.0 g of thiourea were added into 70 mL of deionized water, and ultrasound was carried out for 10 min at room temperature. The mixture was transferred to a 100 mL PTFE lined stainless steel autoclave and react at 160 °C for 12 h. After the products were cooled to room temperature, they were centrifugally washed with distilled water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

6.0 g of melamine were dispersed and stirred in 120 mL of distilled water for 2 h at the room temperature, followed by dring at 60 °C overnight. With a heating rate of 5 °C min−1, the dried mixture was calcined at 500 °C for 2 h and then at 550 °C for another 2 h to obtain the pristine g-C3N4. The g-C3N4/C was prepared by the same procedure as that for pristine g-C3N4 with the addition of PSP to the precursor.

0.2 g of SnS2 and 1.8 g of g-C3N4/C were added into 50 mL ethylene glyol for ultrasonic treatment of 3 h and then transferred to 100 mL PTFE lined stainless steel autoclave for 8 h after heating at 120 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the product was centrifugally washed with distilled water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain the SCC. By varying the amount of added PSP (0.45 g and 0.6 g), the SCC composites with more weight content of carbon were fabricated, which were hereafter denoted as SCC2 and SCC3, respectively.

0.05 g of photocatalyst and 100 mL deionized water (proton source) were put into a quartz reactor. Then, CO2 (99.999%) was injected into the reactor for 20 min without illumination to achieve saturation. CO2 photoreduction evaluations were performed at 25 °C under 300 W Xe-lamp for 5 h, during which a gas chromatograph (GC5890, Nanjing Kejie instrument) with a FID detector was used to detect and quantify the gas products per hour.

Structural characteristics of samples were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), Raman spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), N2-sorption, CO2 and CO temperature-programmed desorption (CO2–, CO-TPD), respectively. Photoelectrochemical features of samples were measured by ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS), photoluminescence (PL) spectra, transient fluorescence (FL), transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), electron spin resonance (ESR), respectively. Reaction intermediates during CO2 photoreduction were record by diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform (DRIFT). These characterization details were described in
Supporting Information
.

Theoretical calculations for binding structures, binding energies of adsorbate and free energy of samples were proceeded using the Vienna ab initio simulation package, generalized gradient approximation with the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof functional, and density functional theory (DFT). The related parameters and calculation details were presented in
Supporting Information
.

Fig. 1
a shows the XRD patterns of the as-prepared g-C3N4, SnS2, g-C3N4/C, and SCC. Two significant diffraction peaks located at 13.23° and 27.35° are indexed to the (100) inplane of tri-s-triazine repeated units and (002) crystal plane of conjugated aromatic interlayer stacking for g-C3N4 (JCPDS No. 87–1526), respectively, matching well with those of the previously-reported pristine counterparts [27]. Compared with the pristine g-C3N4, both the (100) and (002) peaks of g-C3N4/C remain unchanged except the decreases of intensities, which implies that the introduction of carbon preserves the thematic structure of g-C3N4 well but partially destroys the ordered structure. The diffraction peaks of SnS2 locate at 2θ = 15.0°, 28.2°, 32.2°, 41.8°, 46.1°, 50.0°, 52.4°, 54.9°, 60.7°,67.3°and 70.4°, corresponding to (001), (100), (101), (102), (003), (110), (111), (103), (201), (202), and (113) planes (JCPDS No. 83–1705) [28], respectively. As for the SCC, there is a clear intention that both (100) and (002) of g-C3N4 have a slight shift to high angles and the diffraction peak intensity of SnS2 increases, which might be the existence of weak Van der Waals forces between the semiconductors caused by the formation of heterojunction, resulting in relatively loose lattice stacking of SnS2. FT-IR is conducted to explore the chemical structures of the as-prepared g-C3N4, SnS2, g-C3N4/C, and SCC, as shown in Fig. 1 b. For g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/C, there is a wide absorption region at 3100–3400 cm−1 from the vibration modes of N–H for uncondensed amine groups and O–H for adsorbed water molecule on the surface. Five typical stretching modes of CN heterocycles (1234, 1320, 1410, 1570, and 1635 cm−1), as well as the representative bending vibration of striazine units (808 cm−1), can be clearly identified [29]. For the SCC, the characteristic stretching modes of g-C3N4 is well preserved after carbon and SnS2 are introduced. The peak positions of SCC are slightly red-shifted, which is mainly due to the formation of surface S-N chemical bond by the introduction of SnS2, leading to the significant charge transmission between g-C3N4 and SnS2. Fig. S3 records the Raman spectra of the four samples. For the pristine g-C3N4, there are four main peaks at 472, 706, 981, and 1234 cm−1, respectively, signals of the vibration mode in CN heterocycls [30]. For SnS2, the peak at 312 cm−1 can be assigned to the out-of-plane tensile A1g vibration of Sn-S bond [31]. As expected, the g-C3N4/C has an inherited peak similar as those of the pristine g-C3N4, which indicates that no other species are generated during the pyrolysis of melamine andspore precursor, confirming the preservation of CN heterocyclic structure of g-C3N4 in the g-C3N4/C. As to the SCC, the characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 and SnS2 coexist, however, the peak intensity of Sn-S bond remarkably decreases, which illustrates the presence of Van der Waals forces between g-C3N4 and SnS2, and carbon may become the medium of both semiconductor transmissions. XPS are further investigated for the SCC to determine the bond states and surface electronic status with affirmation of the existence of C, N, S and Sn elements ( Fig. S4 ). As seen in Fig. 1 c-1f, the C 1 s, N 1 s, Sn 3d, and S 2p spectra can be deconvoluted into their indicative bonds revolving around g-C3N4, SnS2 and carbon, such as C − C (284.7 eV), C − S (285.8 eV), and N–C = N (288.7 eV) bonds for C1s [31], C = N–C (399.1 eV), N–(C)3 (400.4 eV), and C–N–H (401.4 eV) for N 1 s [32], Sn 3d3/2 (486.5 eV) and Sn 3d5/2 (495.0 eV) for Sn 3d [33], and S 2p3/2 (161.5 eV) and S 2p1/2 (162.6 eV) for S 2p [34], respectively. These are consistent with the results from XRD and Raman analysis.

The Brunner-Emmet-Tellerspecific surface area (SBET) and porosity analysis display that the SBET of SCC reaches the optimal textural properties ( Fig. S8
and
Table S1 ). The reason for the small SBET increment of SCC (22.2 m2 g−1) is probably due to the blockage of pores in carbon sphere by the loaded g-C3N4 and SnS2. This phenomenon implies that the increase of SBET would not contribute much to the increase of photocatalytic activity for the SCC in our case. It can be found that the SCC has the largest hysteresis loop area and, indicating that SCC has more pores ( Fig. S8
a), while the pore size distribution suggests that the pore size of the SCC is concentrated within 10–30 nm ( Fig. S8
b). The presence of mesopores/macropores is beneficial to mass diffusion/transmission and multiple light reflections, which can enhance utilization of light energy. To explore the effect of CO2 adsorption on the CO2 photoreduction activity, CO2-TPD are performed to monitor the interaction status of CO2 with the as-prepared g-C3N4, SnS2, g-C3N4/C, and SCC. It is well known that the adsorption amount of CO2 molecules at different temperatures determines the strength of interaction between CO2 and the photocatalyst for CO2 photoreduction. The higher temperature is, the stronger interaction is [37]. As shown in Fig. 4
a, all of the samples display a similar broad desorption in the range of 600 ∼ 800 °C, which is the evidence of the strong interaction of CO2 with them. By contrast, the SCC possesses the largest area of desorption peak ( Table S2 ), which may be ascribed to synergistic effect of heterojunction and hollow sphere carbon, facilitating the adsorption of CO2 molecules so that increases the amount of formed stable carbonate (m-CO3 2-), i.e., preferable basic sites for more CO2 adsorption. Moreover, the EPR spectra in Fig. 4 b show the maximum intensity of SCC, indicating there are abundant intrinsic structural defects produced on the SCC compared to the other three samples. This is conducive to adsorbing more CO2 and resulting in a higher photocatalytic activity [38].

UV–vis DRS is measured to obtain the optical absorption information of the as-prepared g-C3N4, SnS2, g-C3N4/C, and SCC, as shown in Fig. S9
a. At ∼ 450 nm, the pristine g-C3N4 possessesa pronounced initial absorption band edge, which displays increasing red-shift for the SCC with remarkably increased capacity, indicating that more light energy can be harvested for utilization by both the hollow sphere structure of SCC and the decoration with SnS2 for photocatalytic reaction. Accordingly, the band gap (Eg) values of these samples are calculated by the Kubelka-Munk formula [39]. As shown in Fig. S9
b, the Eg of pristine g-C3N4 is calculated to be 2.87 eV, consisting with the previous reports [40]. By contrast, the Eg of SCC descends to 2.65 eV, resulting in the increased visible light-harvesting capacity, which is supposed to be favorable for more participation of photogenerated electrons to the CO2 photoreduction in the conduction band (CB) of g-C3N4. Fig. 5
a exhibits transient photocurrent responses (TPR) curves of four samples under the on/off periodic irradiation mode of solar simulator. All the samples appear fast, stable and repeatable photocurrent responses in the instant of light irradiation within 850 s for twelve cycles. The photocurrent value of SCC increases greatly compared to those of others, reflecting the more photogenerated electrons are separated with the holes, i.e., the photogenerated electron-hole (e−-h+) pairs in the SCC. This is stemmed mainly from the scaffold and bridging functions of hollow carbon sphere, which ultimately boosts transfer efficiency of electron through the SCC, and thus reinforcing the photoelectric response. Furthermore, it can be observed in Fig. 5 b that the Nyquist plots radius of the SCC is the smallest, indicating that the lower the charge transfer resistance on it, or in other words, the charge transfer efficiency of the SCC is the highest, resulting in the excellent separation efficiency of photoexcited e--h+ pairs. PL spectra are further employed to analyze the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers. As seen in Fig. 5 c, the pristine g-C3N4 and SnS2 exhibit strong steady-state PL intensities under the excitation wavelengths of ∼ 470 nm and ∼ 500 nm, respectively. By contrast, the g-C3N4/C displays a severely decreased PL intensity, verifying that the conductive carbon sphere can serve as the electron transfer channel. For the SCC, there are almost not any PL peak(s) discernible, further solidifying the good match of g-C3N4/C with SnS2 in structure for effective reduction of recombination of photogenerated e- and h+. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay spectra in Fig. 5 d show that the fitted average lifetime of photogenerated charge carries in SCC is 3.36 ns, evidently more prolonged than those of the pristine g-C3N4 (1.72 ns), SnS2 (2.07 ns), and g-C3N4/C (2.51 ns), as listed in Table S3 . The SCC owns the longer e- and h+ lifetime, which means the increased probability of participating in the photocatalytic reaction. These results reveal that the SCC can effectively inhibit the recombination of photogenerated e--h+ pairs, and collaborating well with those of the steady-state luminescence spectra.

The activities of the as-prepared g-C3N4, SnS2, g-C3N4/C, and SCCs as photocatalysts for CO2 reduction are evaluated under irradiation of a 300 W Xe-lamp without adding any sacrificial agent and photosensitizer, in which CO is the sole gas-phase product and no liquid-phase product is detected, suggesting the high selectivity of the photocatalysts. Because the reducing agent for CO2 reduction is water in this work, which could be split into O2 and H2 in the presence of photocatalyst, and compete with photoreduction of CO2. So we also monitor the yields of H2 and O2 during the photoreduction reaction simultaneously. It is found in Fig. 6
a that CO yield of the pristine g-C3N4 is only 37.20 μmol∙g−1 after 5 h irradiation, while the value for g-C3N4/C is 80.38 μmol·g−1, highlighting the promotional role of the carbon spheres. Obviously, the SCC delivers the highest CO yield up to 204.29 μmol·g−1, corresponding an evolution rate of 40.86 μmol·g−1·h−1. This is superior to that of the pristine g-C3N4 (5.5 times), even exceeds those of the reported g-C3N4-based photocatalysts ( Table S4 ). Meanwhile, the O2 and H2 yield are 108.71 and 8.37 μmol·g−1, respectively. The yield ratio of CO to O2 in stoichiometry is therefore calculated to be 1.88 (204.29/108.71), suggesting most of the photoexcited electrons on SCC react for CO2 reduction not H2O reduction. After four cycles, the final yield of CO only drops slightly to 198.82 μmol·g−1, indicating the excellent photo-stability of SCC. The structure of used SCC has no significant changes compared with that of the fresh one, suggesting its good structural stability against photo-corrosion ( Fig. S10 ). By increasing the amount of added spore precursor in the synthesis of SCC, the obtained-SCC2 and SCC3 with higher contents of carbon present a activity-decaying trend (Fig. 6 b), which arises possibly from the overwhelming coverage of the formed g-C3N4 and SnS2, inhibiting the light absorption and utilization, cutting down the overall active sites, and subsequently deteriorate the yield. In addition, CO-TPD measurements are performed to acquire the chemisorption process of the product involved during the CO2 reduction process (Fig. 6 c). Compared to the other three samples, the lower onset desorption temperature and more adsorption capacity of CO in SCC disclose that CO can be more easily deviated from its surface, which is the clear evidence of efficient and selective production of CO [41]. Control experiments show that no any product is detected with only Ar as the feed gas or with SCC under dark condition, indicating the light source, reactant and photocatalyst is indispensable to the CO2 photoreduction reaction. When Ar is used to as feedstock gas instead of CO2, the yield of H2 increases and no CO is detected, implying that the yielded CO originates from the CO2 photoreduction rather than from the oxidation of biomass-derived carbon in the SCC. To further verify this point, an isotopically labeled 13CO2 experiment is performed with a gas chromatography-mass spectrometry to identify if the product of CO originates from the CO2 photoreduction of SCC. As shown in Fig. 6 d, a strong signal can be detected at m/z = 29 assigned to 13CO, well confirming the source of the generated CO is just the fed 13CO2 not the carbon.

To determine the active species during the CO2 photoreduction, ESR measurements are carried out under light irradiation with a 300 W Xe-lamp to identify the presence and effect of superoxide radical (•O2 –) and hydroxyl radical (•OH) in the g-C3N4, SnS2, g-C3N4/C, and SCCs, in which 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) is used as the spin-trapping reagent, as shown in Fig. 7 . Distinctly, there is no diffraction peak in dark, however, when the light is turned on, the characteristic peaks in the four samples appear rapidly, corresponding to DMPO-•O2 − and DMPO-•OH with a conventional 1:2:2:1 quartet mode [42]. The •OH peak intensity of pristine g-C3N4 is weaker than that of pristine SnS2 while the analogy for •O2 – presents the opposite trend, indicating the typical Z-scheme electron transfer mechanism [43]. Moreover, the introduction of carbon and SnS2 significantly increase the peak strength of two free radicals, elucidating the redox capacity of the ternary SCC is better than those of the constitutive g-C3N4, SnS2, and g-C3N4/C, which can endow it higher photocatalytic performance.

In order to research the mechanism on CO2 photoreduction in the SCC, the valence band (EV) and conduction band (EC) of the pristine g-C3N4 and SnS2 are determined by both XPS valence band spectra and the determined Eg values from UV–Vis DRS. Fig. S11 depict the VB plots of g-C3N4 and SnS2, making tangent lines to 1.19 and 1.62 eV, respectively. According to the equation of Eg = EV- EC, the EC values of g-C3N4 and SnS2 are therefore converted to −1.68 and −0.46 eV, respectively. In addition, UPS measurement is performed to determine the charge flow and the energy level of the formed g-C3N4/SnS2 heterojunction in SCC. Since carbon is only a conductor, the presence of carbon does not affect the generation and transfer of photoelectrons between the g-C3N4 and SnS2, and the g-C3N4/C is preliminarily synthesized and then integrated with SnS2 to prepare the SCC in our case, so we measure the work function of g-C3N4/C and SnS2. As shown in Fig. 8
a and 8b, the upper and secondary onsets for g-C3N4/C can be found as 17.15 and 2.82 eV, respectively, while those of the SnS2 locate at 16.92 and 2.89 eV, respectively. The work functions of them are deduced according to the equation: φ = 21.22-ΔE, where 21.22 eV is the excitation energy of He I UPS spectra and ΔE stands for the energy distance from the upper to secondary onset. As a result, the work function of g-C3N4/C is calculated to be 6.89 eV and that of SnS2 is 7.13 eV vs vacuum, respectively, which means the g-C3N4/C possesses a higher Fermi energy level and a smaller work function. When the g-C3N4/C is integrated with SnS2, the electrons would actively transfer from the g-C3N4/C with the relatively small work function to SnS2 with the relatively one to establish a new equilibrium. After the Fermi levels of both reach equilibrium, an internal electric field (IEF) is formed at the interface of g-C3N4/C and SnS2, leading to downwards bent energy band of SnS2 and upwards bent energy band of g-C3N4. The resulting potential variation changes the width of the space charge layer, which creates a strong driving force for the separation of photogenerated e- and h+, and sequentially provides more electrons for usage [44]. When light is irradiated on the surface of SCC, the IEF generated in the heterojunction will accelerate the transfer of photogenerated electrons from the CB of SnS2 to the VB of g-C3N4 through the conductive carbon [45]. Based on the ESR analysis, the electrons transfer mode in the SCC meets the requirement on the Z scheme system. Hence, it is easy to deduce the sketch of charge transfer for the SCC, as shown in Fig. 8 c. By DFT calculation plotted in Fig. 8 d and 8e, the band gaps of g-C3N4 and SnS2 are 1.40 and 1.22 eV, respectively, smaller than the experimental values (2.87 and 2.08 eV). This is caused by the limitations of DFT method itself. The calculation of band structure is sensitive to the change of lattice structure, which results in a lack of computational accuracy when the selected lattices are optimized. Such computational operation often neglects the correlation between the excited state electrons, which therefore makes the calculated value smaller than the experimental value. Many previous reports have similar deviations [46]. But the discrepancy in values between the experiment and theoretical calculation does not affect the qualitative analysis of the results. Both show the well-match between g-C3N4 and SnS2 in energy band structure, guaranteeing the resulting SCC a thermodynamic feasibility for IEF construction with the Z-scheme charge separation.

The intermediates generated during the CO2 photoreduction are often the clear evidence to understand the possible reaction route, in situ DRIFTS is therefore measured to detect the potential intermediates upon the SCC, as shown in Fig. 9
a. During the light irradiation, many conventional intermediates are detected，including HCO3 – (1230 cm−1) [47], H2O (1651 and 1635 cm−1) [48], b-CO3 2- (1321 and 1508 cm−1) [49], and m-CO3 2- (1295, 1395 and 1459 cm−1) [50]. In addition, three absorbance peaks at 1521, 1542, and 1577 cm−1 are found, which are assigned to COOH* together, a crucial intermediate for the formation of CO [51]. After irradiation of 120 min, the main characteristic peaks remain stable and tend to increase, which means the SCC has good activity and stability during CO2 photocatalytic reduction. According to the experimental results, the reduction process can be inferred as the following steps (Scheme 1 ).

Initially, CO2 molecules are adsorbed and activated to CO2* on the surface of SCC, meanwhile some of them react with water molecules to generate H2CO3. Under the Xe-lamp irradiation, the electrons and holes are excited and separated on the SCC, during which the electrons with high reducing capacity retain on the CB of g-C3N4, and the holes with high oxidizing capacity stay at the VB of SnS2. Next, CO2* reacts with H+ (from dissociation of H2CO3) and e- to form the crucial intermediate of COOH*. Then, the COOH* is further reduced to CO* by the e- and H+. Finally, the formed CO* desorb from the surface of g-C3N4 and transform into CO molecules. In order to explore the change in energy for CO2 photoreduction process on SCC, binding energies of an adsorbate (Eb) are calculated by DFT based on the equation of Eb = Eads/sub–(Esub + Eads), where Eads/sub is the total energy of the substrate with an adsorbate on it, Esub and Eads are the total energies of the bare slab and the free adsorbate, respectively [52]. According to the definition, the more positive the binding energy is, the more favorable adsorption the photocatalyst has. Since the whole reaction process follows the Z-scheme mechanism, CO2 will be reduced on the g-C3N4 layer (CNL), which is the major adsorption layer (active sites), while the CO2 adsorption at other layers (SnS2 and carbon) is regarded as non-major adsorption. So we compare the adsorption energies of CO2 on the CNL of pristine g-C3N4 and SCC. Apparently, CO2*, COOH* and CO* are found to have the strongest adsorption energies for the SCC ( Fig. 9 b), which extremely facilitates the further conversion of the reaction to CO without desorption. Hence, the SCC enhances the stability of each reaction intermediate compared with the pristine g-C3N4, achieving high CO activity and selectivity. These results are good coherence to the CO2-TPD experimental data. Based on these results, the reaction pathways of CO2 on the active sites of pristine g-C3N4 and SCC can be proposed, as displayed in Fig. 9 c and 9d. The top view of each structure of reaction pathways is displayed in Fig. S12 and S13 accordingly. In detail, the CNL first adsorbs CO2 molecule and makes it to active to generate the CO2*-CNL structure. Afterwards, the crucial intermediate of COOH* is formed with the assistant of e- and H+ charge transfer (COOH*-CNL). After the second proton-coupled transfer is completed, COOH* will be photoreduced to CO* (CO*-CNL), then eventually desorbs from the CNL. The reaction pathways deduced by DFT are in good accordance with the in situ DRIFTS results. Through the investigation of SCC reaction Gibbs free energy, the reasons for the improvement of photocatalytic performance of SCC is further revealed. As calculated, the free energies of CO2*, COOH*, and CO* for pristine g-C3N4 are 4.11, 0.96, and 1.07 eV, respectively, while for g-C3N4 of the SCC, they are 19.77, −2.73 and 0.68 eV, respectively. From the point of thermodynamic, it is widely recognized that the formation of COOH* is the rate-determining step during CO2 photoreduction. As shown in Fig. 9 e, the SCC has a lower barrier energy for the formation of COOH* than that for the pristine g-C3N4, suggesting the easier formation of COOH* on the SCC, thereby facilitating the conversion of CO2. Furthermore, the SCC has the weakest adsorption capacity for CO, demonstrating that CO is more easily desorbed from the surface of SCC. All the above results show that the hollow spherical Z-scheme heterostructure of SCC can effectively increase the catalytic active site for CO2 adsorption activation, promote charge carrier separation, and enhance photoreduction activity.

According to the above analysis on energy band structure, electron transport pathway, and CO2 reduction process in the SCC, a schematic diagram of mechanism on photocatalytic reduction of CO2 can be proposed in Fig. 10 . In the process of CO2 reduction by the SCC, if electrons are transferred in line with the traditional type-II mechanism, the photogenerated electrons on g-C3N4 will be transferred to the CB of SnS2. However, the EC of SnS2 is determined to be −0.46 eV in this work, greater than −0.53 eV of theoretical reduction potential for CO2-to-CO and insufficient to push the reduction of CO2 to CO. Therefore, the Z-scheme mechanism testified by the ESR results makes sense for the SCC with greatly enhanced separation of electrons and holes (the red arrow route). Under light irradiation, both g-C3N4 and SnS2 are excited to generate abundant electrons and holes. Through the conductive carbon sphere, the electrons on the CB of SnS2 are quickly transferred to the VB of g-C3N4 and then combine with the photogenerated holes on it. The generated repulsive force from the IEF inhibits the transfer of photogenerated holes on the VB of SnS2. Therefore, the electrons accumulate on the CB of g-C3N4 and the holes are left on the VB of SnS2. Then, the well-separated electrons and holes participate in the reduction of CO2 molecules into CO and the oxidation of H2O into O2, and achieve a high photocatalytic activity.

In summary, we have successfully constructed a SnS2/g-C3N4 Z-scheme heterostructure assisted with a hollow spherical biomass-derived carbon (denoted as SCC) by means of a morphology-inherited strategy. The introduction of hollow carbon spheres promote the utilization efficiency of light energy, increase the dispersion of SnS2/g-C3N4 for more reactive active sites, and strengthen the transfer capability of photogenerated electrons. In term of produced CO for CO2 photoreduction, the SCC exhibits a high evolution rate up to 40.86 μmol·g−1·h−1, 5.5 times of the pristine g-C3N4 and also superior to those of many reported g-C3N4-based counterparts. Experimental and theoretical calculations results confirm that the enhancement on the photocatalytic activity of the SCC is attributed to the construction of Z-scheme heterostructure, the present of IEF from g-C3N4 to SnS2, and decreased energy barrier for the formation of crucial COOH* during CO2 reduction. This work provides a meritorious instance to design and construct g-C3N4-based photocatalysts with optimal structural and photoelectrochemical features for efficient carrier transfer and enhanced photocatalytic activity.
